paper:867770771529793903.en_title: Correlation energy of the paramagnetic electron gas at the thermodynamic limit
paper:867770771529793903.research_categories: ["Chemical Sciences", "Physical Chemistry", "Physical Sciences", "Atomic, Molecular and Optical Physics"]

variables.content:
For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

The proposition: For Fermi liquid parameters $F_l$ (or $F_l/m^{*}$) of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) computed in square periodic simulation cells containing $N$ electrons, the leading systematic finite-size correction arising from long-range correlation effects scales asymptotically as $N^{\gamma}$ with exponent $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., a correction term proportional to $N^{-1/4}$), so that finite-$N$ results can be extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting to the form $F_l(N)=c+aN^{-1/4}$; this scaling is the same as that argued in the theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation finite-size effects (Holzmann et al.).

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG), the leading finite-size error in Fermi-liquid-derived quantities such as the angular-momentum Fermi liquid parameters F_l or the Fourier-projected quantities F_l/m^{*} behaves asymptotically as a power law in the finite-system particle number N with exponent -1/4; equivalently,
      \Delta F_l(N) \equiv F_l(N)-F_l(\infty) \propto N^{-1/4},
      so that fitting computed F_l(N) values to the form c + a N^{-1/4} yields an estimate of the thermodynamic-limit intercept c = F_l(\infty). This statement adopts the theoretical asymptotic exponent \gamma = -1/4 predicted for long-range-correlation-dominated finite-size errors.
      Refs. 13,14

For a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) simulated in a periodically repeated finite simulation cell containing N electrons, let E_N denote the computed energy per electron of that N-electron finite system and let E_∞ denote the energy per electron in the thermodynamic limit (infinite system size). The proposition is: the dominant many-body finite-size bias in the energy per electron for the fluid phase falls off for large N as a single leading asymptotic term proportional to N^{-5/4}, i.e., E_N = E_∞ - c N^{-5/4} + o(N^{-5/4}) for some constant c, and furthermore this asymptotic N^{-5/4} dependence provides a quantitatively valid description for the finite system sizes employed in the reported calculations (specifically for system sizes up to about N = 162), so that fitting finite-N results to that leading form yields a meaningful extrapolation to E_∞.
      N.D. Drummond et al., Phys. Rev. B 78, 125106 (2008)

The computed quasiparticle effective mass m* for paramagnetic 2D-UEL exhibits a systematic dependence on the simulation-cell electron number N that is empirically well described by the form m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} for some constant β; applying this N^{-3/2} empirical scaling to finite-N VMC and DMC data (for example N = 146, 218, 302) permits controlled extrapolation to estimates of m*(∞) in the thermodynamic limit. Observationally in the reported datasets, finite-size errors of m* are smaller in VMC than in DMC and the finite-size errors grow as the density is reduced (larger r_s).

Twist-averaged diffusion Monte Carlo (TA DMC) total energies per electron E(N) computed for the three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (HEG) in face-centered-cubic simulation cells with Ewald interactions at particle counts N = 130, 226, and 338 were extrapolated to infinite system size by fitting the linear finite-size model E(N) = E(∞) + b/N (where E(∞) is the thermodynamic-limit energy per electron and b is a scalar coefficient), and the resulting thermodynamic-limit DMC total energies per electron include, for r_s = 0.5, E_DMC(∞) = 3.4254(1) Ha/elec. (this dataset) compared with the prior DMC value 3.4301(1) Ha/elec. (Spink et al.), and for r_s = 1.0, E_DMC(∞) = 0.58640(2) Ha/elec. (this dataset) compared with 0.58780(1) Ha/elec. (Spink et al.); the extrapolation employed numerical 1/N scaling motivated by analytic finite-size considerations but did not apply analytic finite-size correction formulas directly.

The finite-system electron numbers used in the calculations—N = 26, 50, 74, 114 for paramagnetic HEGs and N = 29, 57, 101 for ferromagnetic HEGs—provide multiple system sizes at each density and spin polarization such that the computed values F_l^{s,a}/m^{*}(N) display the onset of finite-size behavior consistent with long-range-correlation-dominated scaling (for example an N^{-1/4} trend), and therefore these system sizes were treated as sufficient for attempting an extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit using the assumed N^{-1/4} dependence.
